chunks.text: In 1856, in a small monastery of the Austrian Empire
a monk began to plant pea seeds.
His name was Gregor Mendel.
Mendel paid attention to the trait of pea plants — their height
the color of the seed, the shape of the seed.
For many years
he crossbred tens of thousands of plants and recorded each result with care.
When he crossed a tall parent with a short parent, the offspring were all tall.
But when he crossed those tall offspring among themselves
short plants appeared again in the next generation.
The ratio was about three to one.
From this observation
Mendel reasoned that a trait was inherited from parent to offspring through two " elements.
" One was a dominant element that appeared on the surface
the other was a recessive element that was hidden.
In 1865, he presented his discovery to a local scientific society.
But its importance was hardly understood by the scientist of the time.
His paper lay buried in dust for decades.
At the start of the twentieth century, three independent researcher rediscovered Mendel's law.
His experiment finally came to be recognized as the starting point of modern genetics.
In a quiet monastery
a single monk who kept facing his pea plants was the first to bring the light of science to the way life is inherited.
